result:
:::writing
There’s a particular kind of quiet that only shows up in between decisions. Not the peaceful kind you get in a forest, or the heavy kind that sits in a late-night room, but a suspended quiet—the kind that hovers just before something becomes something else. It’s the pause between clicking “send” and seeing “delivered,” between stepping onto a train and feeling it lurch forward, between realizing you could change your life and actually doing it. That quiet doesn’t feel like rest. It feels like being held midair.

I think people underestimate how much of life is made of these in-between spaces. We like to tell stories in clean lines: this happened, then that happened, and then everything changed. But the truth is messier. Most of the time, we’re not changing—we’re hovering. We’re considering, hesitating, circling, revisiting the same thought from slightly different angles as if one of those angles will finally unlock something definitive.

Take something small, like deciding whether to text someone. Not a dramatic confession, not a life-altering message—just a simple “hey.” You might think that’s trivial, but it can stretch into hours, even days. You wonder how it will be received, whether it’s too late, whether it’s too early, whether it matters at all. In that stretch of time, nothing externally changes. The world keeps moving. But internally, there’s a whole weather system forming and dissolving over and over again.

That’s the quiet I’m thinking about. The one filled with invisible motion.

It’s strange how much weight we give to outcomes compared to processes. We celebrate decisions as if they’re the only meaningful moments, but decisions are just the visible tip of a much larger structure. Underneath them are layers of doubt, instinct, memory, fear, desire—all negotiating with each other. By the time you decide something, the real work has already been done in those unseen negotiations.

And yet, we’re impatient with that part. We call it overthinking. We tell ourselves to “just do it,” as if clarity can be forced by sheer will. Sometimes that works. Sometimes action creates clarity. But other times, rushing through the in-between space feels like skipping pages in a book and then wondering why the ending doesn’t land.

There’s also a discomfort in not being able to explain where you are. People understand decisions. They understand results. It’s easy to say, “I’m moving,” or “I quit,” or “I’m starting something new.” It’s much harder to say, “I’m thinking about maybe changing something, but I don’t know what yet.” That doesn’t fit neatly into conversation. It doesn’t feel solid enough to present.

So we often keep those spaces private. We carry them quietly, even though they take up a lot of room inside us.

I wonder if that’s why time can feel so inconsistent. A single day can feel long if you’re stuck in that suspended state, weighing possibilities that don’t resolve. Meanwhile, entire weeks can disappear when you’re moving decisively from one thing to the next. It’s not that one contains more events than the other—it’s that uncertainty stretches perception. It asks more questions than certainty ever does.

There’s a kind of honesty that shows up in those moments too. When you don’t know what to do, you can’t fully hide from yourself. You see your patterns more clearly: what you avoid, what you chase, what you pretend not to care about but actually do. Decisions can sometimes disguise those things because they give the illusion of resolution. But indecision exposes them.

For example, if you’re trying to choose between staying where you are and leaving, the question isn’t just logistical. It becomes personal in ways that are harder to articulate. What are you willing to risk? What kind of discomfort feels tolerable? What version of yourself are you trying to become, and which one are you afraid of losing? Those questions don’t have quick answers, and they don’t sit still. They shift depending on your mood, your energy, even the time of day.

Morning optimism might tell you to leap. Evening doubt might tell you to stay.

And both voices can feel equally convincing.

That’s another interesting thing: we like to imagine that clarity is singular, that when you “know,” you’ll know in a clean, unmistakable way. But often, clarity comes in fragments. You might feel certain for a moment, then uncertain again. It’s less like a switch flipping and more like a signal flickering in and out. Over time, those flickers can add up to something steady—but in the moment, they don’t feel reliable.

So we wait. Or we oscillate. Or we distract ourselves.

Distraction is probably the most common response to that kind of quiet. It’s easier to fill the space than to sit in it. Scroll something, watch something, start something else entirely. Not because those things are inherently bad, but because they give you a temporary sense of direction. They replace the question with a sequence: next episode, next post, next task.

But the original question doesn’t disappear. It just waits.

Sometimes it resurfaces unexpectedly, like when you’re doing something mundane—washing dishes, walking somewhere familiar, staring out a window without really meaning to. And suddenly, there it is again, exactly where you left it, as if no time has passed at all.

There’s something almost stubborn about unresolved thoughts. They don’t expire just because you ignore them.

Still, not all in-between spaces are uncomfortable. Some of them are quietly hopeful. There’s a certain openness in not having decided yet. Possibility hasn’t collapsed into a single path. Multiple versions of the future are still viable, still breathing. That can be disorienting, but it can also be expansive.

It means you’re not locked in.

I think that’s why people sometimes linger in these spaces longer than they need to. Not out of fear alone, but out of a reluctance to close off alternatives. Choosing one thing inevitably means not choosing others, and there’s a subtle grief in that, even when you’re choosing something good.

So we hover a little longer. Just in case.

Eventually, though, something gives. Not always dramatically. Sometimes the decision doesn’t feel like a bold step—it feels like a quiet tilt. One option starts to feel slightly heavier than the others, slightly more real, slightly harder to ignore. And you follow that weight, almost without realizing that a decision has been made.

Then the quiet changes.

It doesn’t disappear, but it shifts. It becomes the kind of quiet that comes after movement—the kind where you’re adjusting, recalibrating, noticing what’s different now. There are new questions, of course, but they’re shaped differently. They’re less about “what if” and more about “what now.”

And somewhere in that shift, the suspended feeling releases. You’re no longer midair. You’re in motion again, even if the direction isn’t perfectly clear.

Maybe that’s all we’re really doing, over and over: moving, pausing, hovering, deciding, moving again. Not in a straight line, but in a pattern that only makes sense when you zoom out far enough to see it.

Or maybe it never fully makes sense, and that’s okay too.

Either way, that quiet—the one between decisions—isn’t empty. It’s full of work we don’t always recognize, shaping outcomes we’ll later pretend were obvious. And even if it feels uncomfortable while you’re in it, it’s doing something necessary.

It’s where change gathers itself before it arrives.
:::writing

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
